content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA containing NNN integers. The integers of the array can be positive, negative, or even zero.

Chef allows you to choose at most KKK elements of the array and multiply them by −1-1−1.

Find the **maximum** sum of a subsequence you can obtain if you choose the elements of the subsequence optimally.

**Note**: A sequence aaa is a subsequence of a sequence bbb if aaa can be obtained from bbb by deletion of several (possibly, zero or all) elements. For example, [3,1][3,1][3,1] is a subsequence of [3,2,1][3,2,1][3,2,1] and [4,3,1][4,3,1][4,3,1], but not a subsequence of [1,3,3,7][1,3,3,7][1,3,3,7] and [3,10,4][3,10,4][3,10,4]. Note that empty sequence is also a subsequence.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N,KN, KN,K.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2,..., A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer - the maximum sum of a subsequence you can obtain.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K — the size of the array and the maximum number of elements you may multiply by -1. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum possible sum of a subsequence after performing at most K sign flips.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Flip -10 and -4 to become 10 and 4, then take subsequence [6,10,4,2] → sum 22.\nTest case 2: No flips allowed, all numbers are negative, best is empty subsequence → sum 0.\nTest case 3: No need to flip, take all positive numbers → sum 6.

hidden.label: edge case with zeros and no negatives

solution.approach:
For each test case, the subsequence can be any subset of the array after we optionally flip the sign of up to K elements. Flipping a negative value -x (x>0) to +x allows us to include x in the sum, increasing the total by x. Flipping a non‑negative value never helps because we can simply ignore it. Therefore the optimal strategy is:
1. Add all original positive numbers to the answer (they can always be taken). 
2. Collect the absolute values of all negative numbers. Sort them in descending order. 
3. Flip the K largest (by absolute value) negatives, i.e., add the sum of the first min(K, countNeg) absolute values to the answer.
4. The empty subsequence yields sum 0, but the above sum is never negative, so the result is already optimal.
The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case due to sorting the negatives; this satisfies the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting the negative numbers).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) in the worst case to store the negative values.
